rationale: keys 9 and 10 sort by their 1-byte encoded form (0x09 < 0x0a), not numeric value -- here they coincide, exercised further by mixed-type cases in a later milestone

-24 encodes to 1 byte (0x37); 1000 encodes to 3 bytes (0x1903e8). RFC 8949 SS4.2.1 requires PURE bytewise order with no length pre-sort: 0x19 < 0x37, so the longer encoding of 1000 must sort before the shorter encoding of -24. A length-first (RFC 7049-style) comparator would wrongly sort -24 first, since it mistakes the shorter key for the lexicographically smaller one.

Ten integer keys spanning every encoded width (immediate, 1-byte, 2-byte, 4-byte extensions) plus three negative-integer widths, in one map. Confirms breadth of pure-bytewise ordering across many widths at once, not just a single trap pair. Verified byte-identical across Rust and Go's independent encoders, and Python's, both profiles (no dcbor-specific transform applies to plain integers).

Empty byte string, empty text string, a 1-byte byte-string key, a 1-char text key, and a long text key (past the one-byte length-extension boundary) together in one map -- exercises the shortest possible encoded keys sorting correctly against much longer ones of the same and different major types.

entries.value: a much longer text string key that pushes past the one-byte length extension boundary of twenty three characters

rationale: A tag-wrapped value (tag 2, one of decode-strict's allow-listed tags, wrapping a byte string) used as a map key alongside a plain int key -- confirms the grammar and canonical sort handle a tag value as a key, not just as a top-level or map-value position. Tag 2 wraps the minimal 9-byte big-endian magnitude 2^64 (0x010000000000000000) so the item is a genuinely canonical bignum: this vector round-trips through decode-strict ACCEPT without either an UNKNOWN_TAG or a NON_CANONICAL_BIGNUM false rejection (a native-range magnitude like 0x01 would now be rejected under the bignum rule).
Array values used as map keys, sorted correctly against each other and against a plain int key -- major type 4 (array) sorts after int/negative-int/bytes/text per the canonical major-type ordering.
A map value used as a map key, alongside an int and a text key -- major type 5 (map) sorts after array (major type 4) and before tag (major type 6) per the canonical major-type ordering.

Seven keys spanning six of the seven CBOR major types (int, negative-int, bytes, text, array, map, tag) in one map -- the broadest single cross-major-type ordering exercise in the corpus, confirming the sort holds across the full type range at once, not just pairwise. The tag key wraps the minimal 9-byte magnitude 2^64 (0x010000000000000000) so it is a genuinely canonical bignum and survives decode-strict's NON_CANONICAL_BIGNUM check; its leading 0xc2 byte still sorts it last among the seven keys regardless of payload length.
